did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning-based computer-aided diagnostic (CAD) system named SCaLiNG, which leverages multi-directional compact convolutional neural networks (CNNs) (MobileNet, ShuffleNet, ResNet-18) and Gabor Wavelets for skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel CAD tool named SCaLiNG. It details the methodology involving multi-directional compact CNNs and Gabor Wavelets, describes the experimental setup using the HAM10000 dataset, and reports specific quantitative results (e.g., 0.9170 accuracy) and comparisons with baseline models, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Skin cancer classification leveraging multi‑directional compact convolutional neural network ensembles and gabor wavelets
brief_summary: This study proposes SCaLiNG, a novel computer-aided diagnostic tool for skin cancer classification that leverages multi-directional compact convolutional neural networks and Gabor wavelets. The method decomposes dermoscopic images into directional sub-bands using Gabor wavelets and processes them alongside original images through three parallel lightweight CNNs: MobileNet, ShuffleNet, and ResNet-18. Deep spatial-textural-frequency features extracted from these networks are fused and refined using minimum Redundancy Maximum Relevance feature selection. The model was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which contains seven distinct skin cancer subcategories. Experimental results demonstrated that SCaLiNG achieved a classification accuracy of 0.9170 using only 60 selected features. This performance surpassed conventional single-CNN models, highlighting the tool's potential to assist dermatologists in precise and efficient skin cancer diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin cancer subcategories (melanoma, melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin cancer subtypes using the HAM10000 dataset. The model SCaLiNG predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) using compact CNNs and SVM classifiers, achieving an accuracy of 0.9170.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset in skin cancer image analysis and deep learning research, consisting of more than ten thousand dermatoscopic images depicting seven distinct forms of skin cancer subcategories. The images are labeled by skilled dermatologists and show diversity in patient age, sex, skin type, and lesion location. It is available for free on the ISIC archive website and Harvard Dataverse repository.

use.training: The dataset was divided into training and testing sets with a proportion of 70%-30%. The training portion was augmented to raise the number of training images, prevent overfitting, and improve training performance.
use.testing: The dataset was divided into training and testing sets with a proportion of 70%-30%. The testing portion was used to evaluate the classification accuracy of the SCaLiNG model.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Feature selection to identify the most significant features impacting classification accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.method: SVM classifiers with linear, quadratic, and Gaussian kernels
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Multiclass classification of skin cancer subcategories using extracted features.
Optimization method used to train the CNNs.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made based on classification accuracy metrics without statistical significance testing.

Setting I (Textural-Frequency Features f.notes: Setting I used SVM classifiers trained independently using textural-frequency features extracted by individual CNNs from sets of GW images. The accuracy of 84.7% is reported for this setting in the discussion section.

Setting II (Combined Spatial and Textura.notes: Setting II combined spatial deep features from each DL model with integrated textural-frequency features obtained by GW. The discussion states the classification accuracy in setting II is 90.8%.

Setting III (Combined Features with mRMR.notes: Setting III employed mRMR FS to select the most significant features. The SCaLiNG model achieved an overall classification accuracy of 0.9170 using only 60 selected features. This setting is considered the primary result of the study.

Detailed Metrics for Setting III (Quadra.notes: Table 6 reports these metrics for the Quadratic SVM classifier using mRMR FS applied to combined deep features.

Detailed Metrics for Setting III (Linear.notes: Table 6 reports these metrics for the Linear SVM classifier using mRMR FS applied to combined deep features.

Detailed Metrics for Setting III (Gaussi.notes: Table 6 reports these metrics for the Gaussian SVM classifier using mRMR FS applied to combined deep features.

Per-Class Sensitivity (Setting III).notes: Sensitivity values for each class (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc) for the linear, quadratic, and Gaussian SVM classifiers are provided in the text following Table 6.

AUC Performance.notes: The text states that all SVM classifiers achieved an AUC value higher than 0.95.

architecture_used.main_model: SCaLiNG (ensemble of three compact CNNs: MobileNet, ShuffleNet, ResNet-18) combined with Gabor Wavelets (GW) for feature extraction and SVM classifiers for final classification

architecture_details.feature_extraction_method: Deep features extracted from the last fully connected layer of each CNN
architecture_details.feature_integration: Fusion of spatial deep features from CNNs trained on original images and textural-frequency features from CNNs trained on Gabor Wavelet subbands
architecture_details.feature_selection: Minimum Redundancy Maximum Relevance (mRMR) used to select 60 significant features

data_augmentation: Flipping in horizontal and vertical directions (-45 to 45)
Rotating in horizontal and vertical directions (-60 to 60)
training_details.hyperparameters: Not explicitly reported; stated as modified for optimal performance

preprocessing_techniques: Gabor Wavelet (GW) decomposition to generate directional sub-bands (GW 1-4) for textural-frequency feature extraction
Resizing images to 224 × 224 × 3 to align with CNN input layer dimensions

Data augmentation including flipping (horizontal and vertical, -45 to 45 degrees), scaling (0.5 to 1.5), shearing perpendicularly (-50 to 50), and rotating (horizontal and vertical, -60 to 60 degrees)

conclusions: The proposed SCaLiNG CAD tool, which leverages multi-directional compact convolutional neural networks (MobileNet, ShuffleNet, ResNet-18) and Gabor Wavelets, achieved a classification accuracy of 0.9170 on the HAM10000 dataset.
SCaLiNG outperformed conventional single-CNN models and individual Gabor Wavelet subband approaches, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining spatial, textural, and frequency features.

The integration of deep spatial features from original images with textural-frequency features from Gabor Wavelet subbands significantly enhanced diagnostic accuracy compared to using either data source alone.
Feature selection using the minimum Redundancy Maximum Relevance (mRMR) method improved model performance by selecting the most distinguishing features (60 features) while maintaining high accuracy.

The study addresses limitations of current CAD tools that rely on heavy CNN architectures, single models, and exclusive use of spatial information, offering a lightweight and comprehensive feature representation.
SCaLiNG demonstrates potential to assist dermatologists in the swift and precise recognition and classification of skin cancer subcategories, thereby enhancing patient outcomes.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., 6705 melanocytic nevus vs. 115 dermatofibroma cases), potentially biasing performance metrics toward majority classes.
The model lacks external validation; generalizability to diverse patient populations, skin types, or imaging devices is unverified and identified as a key limitation.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported to support the significance of performance improvements over baseline models.
The system omits pre-segmentation, which simplifies the workflow but may reduce precision for small tumors or lesions with subtle boundaries against surrounding skin.
The model does not incorporate patient-specific clinical data (age, gender, skin type), limiting its ability to account for individual patient characteristics in diagnosis.
Clinical deployment challenges, including regulatory approval and integration into dermatologist workflows, are acknowledged but not addressed in the current study.

Performance varies significantly across classes, with lower sensitivity for melanoma (70.8% with Quadratic SVM) compared to benign nevi (97.0%), raising concerns about false negatives for malignant cases.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset for all phases of the experiment. The dataset was split into a 70% training set and a 30% testing set. The training set was augmented to handle class imbalance and prevent overfitting. The CNNs (MobileNet, ShuffleNet, ResNet-18) were retrained using transfer learning from ImageNet weights. The model was evaluated on the held-out 30% test set. No external or cross-dataset validation was performed.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper explicitly states that the model was developed on the HAM10000 dataset and acknowledges that generalizability to diverse patient populations and imaging devices is unverified. It identifies the lack of external validation as a limitation and states that external validation experiments are planned for future work.

training.notes: Used for training the three compact CNNs (MobileNet, ShuffleNet, ResNet-18) via transfer learning. The data was augmented using flipping, scaling, shearing, and rotation.

validation.notes: The paper does not report a separate validation set for hyperparameter tuning or model selection. It mentions modifying hyperparameters for optimal performance but does not specify a validation split.

testing.notes: Used for final performance evaluation of the SCaLiNG model and comparison with other methods.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: Pre-trained weights for MobileNet, ShuffleNet, and ResNet-18 were used as the starting point for transfer learning before retraining on the HAM10000 dataset.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Training data was augmented using flipping (horizontal/vertical, -45 to 45 degrees), scaling (0.5 to 1.5), shearing (-50 to 50), and rotation (-60 to 60 degrees) to raise the number of training images and prevent overfitting.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: 70%-30% train-test split of the HAM10000 dataset.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used. The test set is a held-out portion of the same HAM10000 dataset.

dataset_level_results.notes: Primary result using 60 selected features. Per-class sensitivities: akiec (82.9%), bcc (90.9%), bkl (83.8%), df (87.0%), mel (70.8%), nv (97.0%), vasc (93.7%).
Result using combined deep features with mRMR feature selection.
Result using combined deep features with mRMR feature selection.

generalizability_notes: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., 6705 melanocytic nevus vs. 115 dermatofibroma cases).
The model lacks external validation; generalizability to diverse patient populations, skin types, or imaging devices is unverified.
The authors acknowledge that the performance may not be consistent across various patient groups with different skin colors, tumor forms, or imaging techniques.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported to support the significance of performance improvements.
information_not_reported: Specific counts of images in the training and testing splits (only percentages are given).
Details on how the 70/30 split was performed (e.g., random seed, stratification by class or patient).
A dedicated validation set for hyperparameter tuning or model selection.
Patient-level separation details (whether images from the same patient appear in both train and test sets).
